target.name: Cambium
target.founder: Simon Waddington, Stephan Herrera, Ben Christensen

methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series B → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.9x) to $127M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 6 same-sector + same-stage peers in Robotics & Manufacturing. Range: $300M - $10.0B.

private.name: 1X Technologies
private.rationale: Same sector (Robotics & Manufacturing), same stage (Series B), post-money $10B

private.name: Bedrock Robotics
private.rationale: Same sector (Robotics & Manufacturing), same stage (Series B), post-money $1.75B

private.name: GrayMatter Robotics
private.rationale: Same sector (Robotics & Manufacturing), same stage (Series B), post-money $300M+

data.narrative:
Cambium is a Series B robotics and manufacturing company that has raised $127M to date. The valuation analysis draws on a comparable universe of 9 private peers and 1 public company operating in the same sector at equivalent or proximate stages. The private peer set includes 1X Technologies, Bedrock Robotics, Dexterity, Collaborative Robotics, and Fairmat, with valuations ranging from $350M to $10.0B.

Two methods were applied to triangulate fair value. The stage-progression method, using total capital raised as a proxy base and applying a 1.9x multiplier typical for Series B advancement, implies a valuation of $241M. The peer-median method across six same-sector, same-stage comparables yields a median valuation of $1.1B, with a range spanning $300M to $10.0B. This wide dispersion reflects heterogeneity in capital intensity, market positioning, and funding velocity within the robotics sector.

Triangulating both methods produces a valuation range of $205M (low) to $1.2B (high), with a median of $658M. The stage-progression floor reflects Cambium's capital base and typical step-up dynamics; the peer median reflects market clearing valuations among funded robotics companies. Key uncertainties driving the wide range include the sector's valuation dispersion, unobserved differences in unit economics and market traction relative to named peers, and the binary outcome risk inherent in frontier-stage hardware ventures.
